Recovery mechanisms -
R1: The alarm timeout is set to a time shorter than the application FTTI. This gives time for the Master Safety Core (S/W safety assumption is that Cortex-M7 core 0 is the Master Safety Core) to service fault handler software. It is the responsibility of the system owner to decide on an appropriate reaction.  Example of reactions that may be possible are:  
 - interrogate the source of a fault to determine if it is part of a safety function,
- save status information and trigger a functional reset, 
 - attempt to recover a faulty peripheral IP with soft reset of the IP (e.g. if NCF[52] is triggered by a FlexCAN RAM fault), 
 - attempt to recover by resetting a software resettable domain (e.g. for NCF[91:97] a software reset of LLCE domain can be considered, for NCF[99:101] a software reset of PFE domain can be considered).
If the servicing of fault handler software fails and results in an FCCU timeout the system will be moved to a safe state within the FTTI.
R2: The Master Safety Core directly handles the interrupt upon the fault being reported and at the same time the FCCU asserts the FCCU outputs to safe state the system. 
R3: The FCCU directly asserts the FCCU outputs to safe state the system and triggers a functional reset to recover the fault.

For NCF[0] - A53 lock-step errors, In the case when DBG_RST_MSK_1a or DBG_RST_MSK_0a bits of SRC_0.DEBUG_CONTROL register are asserted then there may be a case when functional reset may not help in recovering from this fault because the cause of lockstep error may be the debug related flops that can be cleared only on destructive reset. In that case the user will need to handle it with a destructive reset. The recommended reaction would be R1 in this case and the Master Safety Core would assert a destructive reset for the device.

For NCF[1] - M7_0 lock-step errors, there may be a case when functional reset recovery from this fault is not possible because the cause of lockstep error may be the debug related flops that can be cleared only on destructive reset. In this case reset reaction will be escalated to destructive reset after the sequences of functional reset.

The recommended reaction is R1, however in some Rare conditions(where the Clocks are completely lost , System buses are in hang state or logic sitting at destructive reset) R1 recovery may not complete. In interrupt sub-routine, further reaction to create the necessary destructive reset or PoR  can be performed to clear up the NCF source.
Impacted NCF list :21,28,47,48,49,59,60,61,62,63,81,82,109,110,111,112

NCF[21] fault is related to the Main reset partition. NCF[22] fault is related to the Cortex-A53 reset partition. NCF[87] fault is related to the Main reset partition. NCF[107] fault is related to the LLCE reset partition.

For details, see the table “PRST fields for individual partitions and cores” in the  S32G3 Reference Manual.
